interest: Equity-oriented civil actors and an education-reform movement that want to eliminate early social sorting rather than merely soften its effects.
value: Equity and social integration (unified general education for all) in tension with excellence/differentiation and parental choice for gifted or ambitious children.
fear: That any reform short of removing the 6/8-year track leaves the structural sorting machine intact, so early selection keeps reproducing elite social separation.
affected: students entering at age 10-12 (especially lower-SES families)
existing 6/8-year gimnázium students, staff and their families
teachers who would need retraining and support systems
middle-class families using early-selection tracks as a mobility strategy
assumption: That the sorting mechanism is the track structure itself, so only structural abolition (not dial-adjustment) can end the sorting, and that the documented reform programme's diagnosis transfers to this decision.
empirical_uncertainty: The causal claim that early tracking drives social separation is well-supported in the literature, but whether abolition alone removes sorting (rather than displacing it into residential/informal channels) is contested and depends on the bundled support systems.
